sources.writer_profile: Vicky Hampton is a British-born, Amsterdam-based food and travel writer who has covered Valencia's restaurant scene across multiple visits. Her writing focuses on honest, experience-driven recommendations for independent travellers, with a particular interest in neighbourhood eating and value-for-money finds. She is the sole author and editor of Amsterdam Foodie.

David Neimanis is a food writer at The Infatuation, an editorial restaurant guide known for its candid, first-person review style. He covers Valencia for The Infatuation's city guides, drawing on in-person visits to evaluate restaurants across neighbourhoods and price points. The Infatuation operates independently of restaurant advertising.

Albert Padilla is the founder and lead writer of Grupo Gastro Trinquet, a Valencia-based gastronomic platform covering the city's restaurant scene in both Spanish and English. His guides are neighbourhood-organised and draw on deep local knowledge of Valencia's dining landscape, from market-side tapas bars to Michelin-recognised dining rooms.

Yolanda Galiana is the founder and chief restaurant critic of Bonviveur, one of Spain's leading independent gastronomic publications. Her Valencia coverage focuses on the city's top-tier dining rooms and nationally recognised chefs, reflecting Bonviveur's positioning as a guide for serious gastronomes. She has been actively reviewing Valencia restaurants through 2026.

Paula Pons is a food journalist and critic at Valencia Plaza, the city's leading independent news and culture outlet. She writes the Guía Hedonista section, covering Valencia's restaurant openings, closures, and dining trends with close attention to the local food scene and its chefs. Her work reflects a deep institutional knowledge of Valencia's gastronomic landscape.

Paco Palanca is the food writer and photographer behind Ojo al Plato, an independent Spanish-language restaurant guide to Valencia organised by district. His neighbourhood-by-neighbourhood approach provides granular coverage of the city's dining scene, from longstanding local institutions to recent openings, with regular 'last visit' updates across multiple years. He is the sole author of the guide.

Paco Palanca is the food writer and photographer behind Ojo al Plato, an independent Spanish-language restaurant guide to Valencia organised by district. His neighbourhood-by-neighbourhood approach provides granular coverage of the city's dining scene, from longstanding local institutions to recent openings, with regular 'last visit' updates across multiple years. He is the sole author of the guide.

restaurants.name: Casa Carmela
restaurants.quote: Their paella is cooked over open wood fire — the rice is sticky with umami goodness, and just charred on the bottom.

restaurants.name: La Pepica
restaurants.quote: Their paella Valenciana is a specialty, and comes according to the traditional recipe with garrofó.

restaurants.name: Casa Roberto
restaurants.quote: Roberto's paella Valenciana came stuffed with all the things you'd expect (chicken, rabbit, beans) but also artichoke and snails.

restaurants.name: Taberna Pare Pere
restaurants.signature_dish: Just-seared tuna fillets with pistachio sauce
restaurants.quote: This simple spot is known for its fish and seafood: think just-seared tuna fillets with pistachio sauce.

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña
restaurants.quote: This place is ultra-traditional, but in the best possible way: mouth-watering jamón Iberico, silky Rioja and steaming piles of mussels.

restaurants.name: La Pilareta
restaurants.quote: One of the more traditional bars in the area is La Pilareta, where the mussel shells are still thrown into crates in front of the bar.

restaurants.name: El Rodamón de Russafa
restaurants.quote: For a modern, creative, fusion version of tapas and wine, head over to the hipster, minimalist El Rodamón de Russafa. On the menu, you'll be greeted with some Asian flavours.

restaurants.name: Timoteo Gastrobar
restaurants.quote: Patatas bravas came with sobrasada – a sort of spicy, cured sausage meat – and the bill at the end was staggeringly reasonable.

restaurants.name: La Tasqueta del Mercat
restaurants.quote: We paid under €25 for a five-course menu plus only a few extra euros for wine — good quality cooking at an excellent price.

restaurants.name: Karak
restaurants.signature_dish: Shrimp ravioli with red curry sauce
restaurants.quote: Modern fine-dining restaurant Karak is the brainchild of the extremely talented Rakel Cernicharo, winner of Top Chef. One of my favourite dishes was the shrimp ravioli with red curry sauce.

restaurants.name: Canalla Bistro
restaurants.quote: Think international fusion dishes that aren't afraid of bold, spicy flavours. Despite Camarena's culinary cachet, dinner at his bistro was extremely affordable.

restaurants.name: La Salita
restaurants.quote: A tasting menu that's both playful and rooted in Valencian flavors, built around vegetables, seafood, and local ingredients from chef Begoña Rodrigo.

restaurants.name: Barbaric
restaurants.quote: An old-school neighborhood spot that feels familiar yet fresh thanks to a killer natural wine list and food that earns every bit of its reputation.

restaurants.name: La Cantina de Ruzafa
restaurants.signature_dish: Giant almuerzo sandwich with cold beer
restaurants.quote: Keeps this tradition alive inside a garden-view space that feels like a family's kitchen — a giant sandwich, a cold beer, a handful of olives or peanuts, and an espresso.

restaurants.name: Tasca Ángel
restaurants.quote: A standing-room-only spot established in 1946, the butterflied sardines are sourced fresh that morning from Mercado Central.

restaurants.name: Ricard Camarena
restaurants.quote: A high-class psychedelic tea party grounded in serious technique, with produce grown in an on-site garden.

restaurants.name: Ostras Pedrín
restaurants.signature_dish: Tempura oysters with tinned fish and gildas
restaurants.quote: A chic, bustling nautical bar serving tinned fish and gildas alongside tempura oysters — a vermut costs €3.

restaurants.name: Goya Gallery
restaurants.quote: A refined, welcoming spot serving some of the city's best seafood and rice dishes in the Eixample neighbourhood.

restaurants.name: Acapulco Bar
restaurants.signature_dish: Hake croquettes in green chili sauce; rabbit al pastor
restaurants.quote: Goes beyond the usual tacos and nachos you'll find at most Mexican spots in Spain, with seasonal local ingredients and dishes like rabbit al pastor.

restaurants.name: Flama
restaurants.signature_dish: Steak tartare; rodaballo a la brasa
restaurants.quote: Go-to for fish and well-cut meat cooked over an open flame, surrounded by polished concrete and a serious wine list — steak tartare and rodaballo a la brasa among the highlights.

restaurants.name: Rausell
restaurants.quote: A family-run institution open since the 1940s where chefs go when they're off duty, working with seven fishmongers and over 100 small producers.

restaurants.name: Central Bar
restaurants.signature_dish: Namesake sandwich (pork loin, caramelized onion, mustard and cheese)
restaurants.quote: A down-to-earth project inside the Mercado Central dedicated to almuerzo: the namesake sandwich with juicy pork loin, caramelized onion, mustard, and cheese, stacked warm.

restaurants.name: Saiti
restaurants.quote: A tiny, six-table neighbourhood restaurant that punches way above its size with a seasonal tasting menu celebrating local ingredients from chef Vicente Patiño.

restaurants.name: El Poblet
restaurants.quote: Transforms local dishes into something surprising without losing soul — chef Luis Valls gives Quique Dacosta's local produce focus a creative, contemporary expression.

restaurants.name: Askua
restaurants.quote: Serves some of the best steak in Spain, with limited cuts sourced from Cárnicas Luismi Garayar, each presented with a ceremonial tableside service.

restaurants.name: Forastera
restaurants.quote: Hyper-focused on seasonal produce, small producers, and dishes that mix bright, coastal flavors without draining your wallet — mains between €25 and €55.

restaurants.name: L'Alquimista
restaurants.quote: A hidden shrine to fresh pasta — the tiny place is intimate, with a terrace soaking perfect afternoon sun, and the air always smells like browned butter and truffle.

restaurants.name: Nozomi
restaurants.quote: Might serve the best sushi in Spain — it happens to be right here in Valencia, with exceptional unagi and a passion for authentic Japanese technique.

restaurants.name: Augusta
restaurants.quote: Housed in a restored early-1900s fisherman's cottage with wood shutters and original tile floors, serving northern Italian comfort food with genuine warmth.

restaurants.name: Tasca Sorolla
restaurants.quote: Just a sizzling plancha, a couple of chalkboards, and Ximo, the owner and chef — daily-fresh seafood and vegetables, no shortcuts.

restaurants.name: Fierro
restaurants.quote: A single table for twelve diners and a tasting menu where Mediterranean ingredients meet Argentine culinary sensibility — one Michelin star and the intimacy of a private supper.

restaurants.name: Fraula
restaurants.quote: Valencia's newest Michelin star recipient as of 2024, Fraula has earned its place at Calle Cirilo Amorós with a market-focused menu that changes constantly with the seasons.

restaurants.name: Kaido Sushi Bar
restaurants.quote: The only Japanese restaurant in Valencia with a Michelin star, offering a pared-down omakase experience with exceptional product sourcing.

restaurants.name: Lienzo
restaurants.quote: Chef María José Martínez received a Michelin star in 2025 for a menu where the art of beekeeping and organic farm produce define the cooking — earthy, inventive, and deeply personal.

restaurants.name: Riff
restaurants.quote: Chef Bernd Knöller — the most Valencian of German chefs — leads a Michelin-starred kitchen with special sensitivity to seafood and a deep passion for Valencian rice dishes.

restaurants.name: Vaqueta Gastro Mercat
restaurants.quote: Un restaurante ideal para disfrutar de tapas para compartir, arroces y fideos en un entorno moderno dentro del casco histórico.

restaurants.name: Casa Baldo 1915
restaurants.signature_dish: Sobrasada ibérica sandwich; bocata de pollo campero
restaurants.quote: Maintains the essence of traditional Valencian cooking adapted to contemporary tastes — the sobrasada ibérica and campero chicken sandwich won a special award at the Amstel Cacau d'Or 2024.

restaurants.name: Pelayo Gastro Trinquet
restaurants.quote: Combines Valencian gastronomic tradition with the sporting culture of pilota valenciana — diners eat while watching matches in the oldest trinquet court in the Comunitat Valenciana.

restaurants.name: 2 Estaciones
restaurants.quote: Cocina cambiante con carácter artesanal y dinamismo creativo — a seasonal Mediterranean bistro offering excellent value with a regularly changing market menu.

restaurants.name: Ampar
restaurants.quote: Readaptación de lo tradicional en conceptos nuevos pero siempre muy comprensibles — traditional Valencian ingredients reinterpreted in accessible contemporary form.

restaurants.name: Habitual
restaurants.quote: Cocina de proximidad — el Mediterráneo de raíz es su nuevo lema. Market-driven cooking from Quique Dacosta's casual bistro concept at the Mercado de Colón.

restaurants.name: Ultramarinos Huerta
restaurants.quote: Tapas de autor y delicatessen in an old delicatessen shop setting — a neighbourhood anchor with excellent produce selection and creative small plates.

restaurants.name: Mundua
restaurants.quote: Cocina que hace confluir en el mismo plato recetas de varias procedencias — cuisine that brings together recipes from several different culinary traditions in a single dish.

restaurants.name: Arrosseria Pierpaolo
restaurants.quote: Los arroces los bordan, primero porque quien se embarca en gestionar una arrocería algo tiene que saber del tema — Italian-Valencian fusion that delivers exceptional rice dishes.

restaurants.name: Agua Salada
restaurants.signature_dish: Fresh prawns and quisquilla served raw
restaurants.quote: Gamba y quisquilla muy fresca, tanto que te ofrecen que las comas crudas y está exquisita — the ultra-fresh seafood is offered raw if you choose, and it's exquisite.

restaurants.name: Bodega Anyora
restaurants.quote: Propuestas tienen una base tradicional pero con un toque creativo que las hace más interesantes — traditional roots with a creative twist that makes every dish more interesting.

restaurants.name: Emiliano Bodega
restaurants.quote: A project born in December 2024 still in consolidation phase — contemporary seafood cooking with Cabanyal's market produce at its heart.

restaurants.name: Cocleque
restaurants.quote: Comer en el restaurante de Maribel es todo un lujo gastronómico, ya que ofrece una materia prima de calidad superior — dining here is a genuine gastronomic luxury thanks to exceptional produce.

restaurants.name: El Cabanyal Restaurant
restaurants.quote: Javier Nuria cocina lo que le apetece y por eso define su cocina como libre — the chef's completely free-spirited approach to contemporary Mediterranean cooking defines this Cabanyal restaurant.

restaurants.name: Gallina Negra
restaurants.quote: Bib Gourmand Michelin: productos de calidad, una cocina con excelente relación calidad-precio — quality products and excellent value-for-money cooking recognised by Michelin's Bib Gourmand.

restaurants.name: La Sastrería
restaurants.quote: La carta es una oda al mar. Los platos son tradicionales, elaborados con producto de proximidad — the menu is an ode to the sea, traditional dishes made with locally sourced seafood.

restaurants.name: Namúa
restaurants.quote: Reinterpretar platos tradicionales es una habilidad que Víctor domina muy bien, con mucho ingenio — chef Víctor excels at reinterpreting traditional Valencian dishes with great creativity.

restaurants.name: Xaruga
restaurants.quote: Un pequeño local inaugurado apenas hace algo más de tres meses con una cocina abierta a la sala — chef Amparo Nacher leads a small, open-kitchen restaurant that opened in 2024.

restaurants.name: Noble
restaurants.quote: Noble presents its offering as a return to traditional recipes — those classics that should never go out of style — under chefs José Cifre and Ángel García.

restaurants.name: Informal
restaurants.quote: Producto de primera, mucho pescado, marisco — Xavi Climent's second Valencia location, focusing on premium seafood and shellfish with a relaxed neighbourhood feel.
